name: Scalable REST API
purpose: A production-ready REST API architecture with managed relational storage, caching, and async message handling. Designed for transactional workloads requiring consistent response times and horizontal scalability.
recommendedFor: Transactional APIs with relational data models
Services with peak traffic variance requiring caching
Applications integrating with downstream async consumers
notRecommendedFor: Analytics or reporting workloads (use Data Pipeline template)
Simple CRUD with no scaling requirements (use Lightweight API template)

services.name: AWS SQS
services.role: Async task queue for background jobs

parameters.label: Cloud provider
parameters.lockedReason: This template is certified for AWS only. Provider cannot be changed.

costDrivers: SQS message volume if queue is enabled
scalingBehavior: RDS scales vertically; ElastiCache supports horizontal read replicas. SQS scales automatically with message volume at no instance cost.
rationale: This template encodes the standard ACME pattern for API services — a relational store for transactional integrity, a cache layer to absorb read traffic, and optional async queuing to offload background tasks. It eliminates setup variance across teams while keeping all services within pre-approved governance boundaries.
tradeoffs: RDS requires schema migrations; not suitable for schema-free workloads
ElastiCache adds operational complexity for cache invalidation strategies
SQS is eventually consistent — not appropriate for synchronous workflows
alternatives: Event-Driven Microservice template — if your API is primarily event-sourced
Lightweight API template — if no caching or queuing is needed

aiInsight.fit: Strong match for most application teams at ACME. All three services are fully pre-approved and proven in production across 14 internal services.
aiInsight.cost: Cost is predictable and scales linearly. The $180–$420 range assumes moderate traffic; caching significantly reduces RDS load and cost at scale.
aiInsight.risk: Low risk. No restricted services. All components are within FinOps-approved tiers. The only operational risk is RDS vertical scaling lag during traffic spikes.
evidence: Zero governance exceptions required in last 12 months

resourcesCreated: RDS PostgreSQL instance (db.t3.medium)
ElastiCache Redis cluster (cache.t3.micro, 1 node)
SQS standard queue (if enabled)
VPC security groups for each service
Parameter store entries for connection strings
actionsPerformed: IAM role binding for application service account
Tagging with team, project, and environment labels

name: Real-Time Analytics Pipeline
purpose: A governed data pipeline architecture combining scalable object storage, a managed data warehouse, and an in-memory cache for low-latency query serving. Built for teams processing large datasets and serving analytical results to downstream consumers.
recommendedFor: Analytics and reporting workloads over large datasets
Data pipelines producing aggregated results consumed by APIs
Teams requiring SQL-queryable data without managing infrastructure
notRecommendedFor: Transactional APIs (use Scalable REST API template)
Teams without a data engineering owner for pipeline maintenance

services.name: GCP BigQuery
services.role: Managed data warehouse for analytical queries

services.name: GCP Cloud Storage
services.role: Raw data ingestion and archival layer

services.name: GCP Memorystore (Redis)
services.role: Query result cache for low-latency serving

parameters.label: Data residency policy
parameters.lockedReason: Data residency is locked by the Data Governance Policy v2. Contact the Platform team to request a residency exception.

costDrivers: BigQuery query costs (per-TB scanned — dominant variable)
Memorystore instance size if cache is enabled
scalingBehavior: BigQuery and Cloud Storage scale automatically with data volume. Costs are query-driven — partitioning and clustering are strongly recommended to control scan costs.
rationale: This template provides a governed GCP analytics stack. BigQuery's serverless query engine eliminates cluster management overhead. Cloud Storage provides a cost-effective landing zone. The optional Memorystore layer serves aggregated results to APIs without re-querying BigQuery on every request.
tradeoffs: BigQuery requires approval — provisioning timeline extends by 1–2 business days
Query costs are variable and require FinOps monitoring and query optimization
Not suitable for sub-second transactional operations
alternatives: Scalable REST API template — if you need a transactional store rather than analytics
Request a custom template via the Platform team for hybrid OLTP/OLAP requirements

aiInsight.fit: Well-suited for analytics and data engineering workloads. BigQuery's approval requirement adds lead time but is consistently approved for data-owner teams.
aiInsight.cost: Cost variability is high. Unoptimized queries can drive significant BigQuery costs. FinOps recommends partitioning tables by ingestion date before go-live.
aiInsight.risk: Moderate. BigQuery requires approval and carries higher cost risk. Data residency locking prevents accidental policy violations.
evidence: BigQuery approval granted in 100% of compliant requests

resourcesCreated: BigQuery dataset (pending approval)
Cloud Storage bucket with lifecycle policy
Memorystore Redis instance (if enabled)
IAM service account for pipeline workload
VPC Service Controls perimeter entry
actionsPerformed: BigQuery approval request submitted to Platform team
IAM role binding for data pipeline service account

name: Event-Driven Microservice
purpose: An async-first microservice architecture using a NoSQL document store and a managed message queue. Designed for services that process events, handle async workflows, and require high write throughput with flexible schema evolution.
recommendedFor: Async event processors and background workers
Services with high write throughput and schema flexibility
Microservices that publish or consume events from other services
notRecommendedFor: Workloads requiring ACID transactions across multiple records
Synchronous request-response APIs with no async component
costDrivers: DynamoDB read/write capacity units or on-demand request volume
SQS message count and data transfer
scalingBehavior: Both DynamoDB (on-demand mode) and SQS scale automatically with traffic. On-demand DynamoDB can absorb sudden burst traffic without pre-provisioning.
rationale: The event-driven template is the lowest-friction path for async microservices at ACME. DynamoDB's on-demand pricing means you only pay for what you use, and SQS provides durable, scalable message delivery with no broker management. Both services are fully pre-approved.
tradeoffs: DynamoDB's single-table design requires upfront access pattern planning
SQS Standard queues do not guarantee ordering — use FIFO for order-sensitive workflows
No relational query support; complex joins require application-level aggregation
alternatives: Scalable REST API template — if relational data integrity is required
Real-Time Analytics Pipeline — if events feed downstream analytics queries

aiInsight.fit: Excellent fit for async-first and event-sourced architectures. The simplest approved template with the lowest baseline cost.
aiInsight.cost: Cost-efficient at low and variable traffic volumes. On-demand DynamoDB eliminates over-provisioning risk.
aiInsight.risk: Low risk. All services are pre-approved with no data residency restrictions. DynamoDB table design is the primary risk factor — poor key design leads to hot partitions.
evidence: Lowest avg monthly cost of all approved templates
No governance exceptions in last 18 months

resourcesCreated: DynamoDB table (on-demand capacity mode)
SQS queue (Standard or FIFO, as configured)
IAM role with least-privilege DynamoDB and SQS policy
Dead-letter queue for SQS (automatically attached)
Parameter store entries for table name and queue URL
actionsPerformed: IAM role binding for service account
Tagging with team, project, and environment labels

name: ML Inference Service
purpose: An architecture for serving trained ML models via a scalable inference endpoint. Combines a managed data warehouse for feature storage, a NoSQL store for prediction caching, and object storage for model artefacts.
recommendedFor: Teams serving trained ML models via synchronous or async endpoints
Inference workloads requiring low-latency feature retrieval
Projects with model artefacts requiring governed storage and versioning
notRecommendedFor: Online model training (not a training pipeline template)
Teams without a defined model deployment and versioning process
Simple rule-based scoring that does not require an ML model

services.name: GCP BigQuery
services.role: Feature store and batch prediction input

parameters.label: Data residency policy
parameters.lockedReason: Data residency is locked by the Data Governance Policy v2. Contact the Platform team to request a residency exception.

costDrivers: Cloud Storage volume for model artefacts
Memorystore instance size if caching is enabled
scalingBehavior: Feature retrieval scales with BigQuery's serverless model. Model artefact storage costs scale with artefact count and size. Inference cache hit rate directly impacts BigQuery query frequency and cost.
rationale: This template provides the standard ACME pattern for operationalizing ML models. Cloud Storage provides versioned, auditable model artefact storage. BigQuery handles feature retrieval at scale without managing a separate feature store. The optional Memorystore layer reduces repeated inference for identical inputs.
tradeoffs: BigQuery approval adds 1–2 business days to provisioning lead time
Real-time feature freshness is limited by BigQuery's batch query model
Inference caching is only effective for high-repeat prediction inputs
alternatives: Real-Time Analytics Pipeline — if the primary output is analytics dashboards rather than model predictions
Contact the ML Platform team for custom streaming inference architectures

aiInsight.fit: Appropriate for teams with an established model training workflow who are ready to operationalize. Requires ML Platform team sign-off alongside BigQuery approval.
aiInsight.cost: Cost depends heavily on feature query volume. Enabling the inference cache and optimizing BigQuery partitioning can reduce costs by 40–60%.
aiInsight.risk: Moderate. BigQuery approval required. ML Platform Policy v1 mandates model artefact retention and audit logging — verify your team's compliance posture before provisioning.
evidence: BigQuery approval granted for all compliant ML use cases
ML Platform Policy v1 compliance rate: 100% for provisioned teams

resourcesCreated: BigQuery dataset for feature store (pending approval)
Cloud Storage bucket with versioning enabled
Memorystore Redis instance (if enabled)
IAM service account with least-privilege ML access
Cloud Storage lifecycle policy for artefact retention
actionsPerformed: BigQuery approval request submitted to Platform team
ML Platform Policy compliance check triggered

name: Lightweight API
purpose: A minimal, cost-effective API stack for simple CRUD services with modest traffic and no caching or async requirements. Provides a managed relational database without the overhead of a full cache and queue layer.
recommendedFor: Simple CRUD APIs with predictable, modest traffic
Internal tooling services and admin dashboards
Prototypes and early-stage services before scaling requirements are known
notRecommendedFor: Services with peak traffic spikes (use Scalable REST API template)
Production services expected to exceed 500 concurrent connections
costDrivers: Cloud SQL instance tier (primary cost driver)
scalingBehavior: Cloud SQL scales vertically. Horizontal read replicas can be added as traffic grows, but this template does not include them by default.
rationale: The lightest approved template for relational workloads. Reduces setup time and cost for services that do not yet need caching or queuing. Designed to graduate to the Scalable REST API template when traffic or complexity increases.
tradeoffs: No caching layer — all reads hit the database directly
No async queue — all processing is synchronous
Not designed for burst traffic; sustained high concurrency will require migration
alternatives: Scalable REST API template — when caching and async processing become necessary
Event-Driven Microservice template — if the workload is write-heavy with schema flexibility

aiInsight.fit: Well-suited for internal services, admin tools, and early-stage APIs with modest and predictable traffic. Not recommended for customer-facing services with unpredictable traffic.
aiInsight.cost: Lowest cost of all approved templates. Predictable and easy to forecast. FinOps Tier-1 policy applies.
aiInsight.risk: Very low risk. Single fully-approved service with no data residency restrictions. Primary operational risk is database connection exhaustion under unexpected traffic.

resourcesCreated: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance
Automated backup policy (7-day retention)
Private IP configuration within project VPC
Parameter store entry for connection string
actionsPerformed: IAM role binding for application service account
Database user creation with scoped permissions
Tagging with team, project, and environment labels

name: High-Performance Data Lakehouse
purpose: A high-throughput data lakehouse architecture combining managed object storage, a serverless query engine, and a dedicated analytics accelerator. Designed for teams processing petabyte-scale datasets with sub-second query requirements. Includes components that are currently restricted for most ACME organisations.
recommendedFor: Petabyte-scale analytics with strict query latency requirements
Teams with dedicated data platform engineering ownership
Workloads requiring real-time and batch query serving from a single store
notRecommendedFor: Standard analytics teams without dedicated data engineering support
Organisations without approved exception for restricted components
Transactional or operational workloads (use Scalable REST API template)
Teams without FinOps approval for high-cost infrastructure

services.name: GCP Bigtable
services.role: Low-latency NoSQL accelerator for real-time query serving

services.name: GCP Dataflow
services.role: Managed stream and batch data processing

parameters.label: Bigtable node count
parameters.lockedReason: Bigtable node count is locked pending exception approval. Contact the Cloud Platform team to request a higher allocation.

parameters.label: Cloud provider
parameters.lockedReason: This template is certified for GCP only. Provider cannot be changed.

costDrivers: Bigtable node hours are the dominant cost — scales with node count and uptime
BigQuery query cost scales with data scanned per query
Storage cost is predictable and low relative to compute
scalingBehavior: Cost scales non-linearly with Bigtable node count and BigQuery scan volume. High variance expected in early stages.
rationale: Designed for organisations with mature data platform teams that need petabyte-scale query performance with real-time serving. This template is intentionally restricted for general use because Bigtable and Dataflow require specialist operational knowledge and carry significant cost exposure without careful management.
tradeoffs: Bigtable adds significant operational complexity compared to Memorystore
Dataflow requires data engineering expertise to manage pipelines safely
Cost variance is high — budget overruns are a real risk without FinOps oversight
Restricted components require exception approval before provisioning can proceed
alternatives: Real-Time Analytics Pipeline template — uses approved components with lower operational overhead
ML Inference Service template — if the primary need is model-serving rather than query acceleration

aiInsight.fit: This template is designed for specialist data platform teams with petabyte-scale needs. It includes components that are currently restricted for most ACME organisations and requires exception documentation before provisioning.
aiInsight.cost: Estimated $480–$1,800 per month with high variance. Bigtable node hours and BigQuery query volume are the primary cost drivers. FinOps review is required before production provisioning.
aiInsight.risk: Two included services — Bigtable and Dataflow — are restricted under current ACME policy. Provisioning will not proceed without a governed exception. Operational complexity is significantly higher than alternative templates.
evidence: GCP Bigtable is classified as restricted under ACME Data Platform Policy v2.1
GCP Dataflow requires specialist pipeline management — not approved for self-service
Historical cost data shows 3x overage risk in first 90 days without FinOps governance
